jokes.punchline: Kramer finds the silver lining: prosthetic squirrel hips are proven effective (because the squirrel could scurry away from the hawk).
jokes.explanation: A brilliant convergence joke. The squirrel's medical saga (tiny instruments, the surgery) pays off with the unexpected revelation that it now has prosthetic hips — and the proof they work is that it scurried away during a hawk attack. 'Prosthetic squirrel hips' is an inspired phrase. Kramer's optimistic reframe of total disaster via an absurdly specific medical validation is peak character comedy. Justifies 8+ for originality, economy, and the satisfying convergence of plotlines.

jokes.text: I'll tell you, it was a grind having to fill 10 hours a day. I'm not sure I was ready to have my own talk-show set.
jokes.setup: The talk show set is destroyed. Kramer reflects on the experiment.
jokes.punchline: 'Fill 10 hours a day' — Kramer was apparently running a 10-hour daily talk show in his apartment. The hubris of the scale combined with the self-doubt is funny.
jokes.explanation: '10 hours a day' is a funny specific detail — Merv Griffin ran long shows, but Kramer was doing 10 hours of programming in a studio apartment. His understated self-doubt after all the hubris is a good character landing.

jokes.text: I got the nut bread, George. Let's go.
jokes.setup: The post-adventure scene — George is stuck with the squirrel.
jokes.punchline: Miranda has bought nut bread for the squirrel — she's fully committed to squirrel care, while George is miserable. 'Let's go' implies George has no choice.
jokes.explanation: Miranda's nut bread purchase is a good character beat — she's invested in the squirrel, and George is trapped. Functional but mild.

jokes.text: So the squirrel's gonna make it? / Yeah, he's in my bed. / I'm sleeping on the couch. / On the couch? So you're... / Still getting nothing.
jokes.setup: George reports on the squirrel's status.
jokes.punchline: George is sleeping on the couch because the squirrel has his bed — and he's 'still getting nothing' from Miranda. The squirrel has displaced him domestically AND romantically.
jokes.explanation: A satisfying callback convergence — George's two problems (getting nothing romantically, squirrel responsibility) have merged: the squirrel is in his bed, Miranda is cold, and George is on the couch. 'Still getting nothing' is a great tag that echoes the earlier usage.

jokes.text: Oh, go ahead, pigeons. Laugh it up.
jokes.setup: George walks out into the street, couch-sleeping, sexless, squirrel-saddled.
jokes.punchline: George accuses the pigeons of laughing at his misfortune — personifying them as gleeful witnesses to his humiliation.
jokes.explanation: A perfect episode button. George concluding that the pigeons are laughing at him — after running them over, breaking the deal, being shamed by a squirrel — is the ideal character closing beat. 'Laugh it up' addressed to urban pigeons is highly quotable and encapsulates George's entire relationship with the universe: everything is conspiring against him, including birds.

jokes.text: I'm getting in my car now, and the last I heard, we have no deal.
jokes.setup: George threatens the pigeons after accusing them of laughing at him.
jokes.punchline: George reasserts the 'no deal' with squirrels — but extends it as a threat toward the pigeons too, as if the deal is now under review.
jokes.explanation: A great final callback — George threatening the pigeons using the 'no deal' contract language from the pigeon subplot. It comes full circle: the deal began with pigeons, was tested by squirrels, and George now weaponizes the no-deal concept against the pigeons. Good structural closing of the pigeon thread.

jokes.text: I'm glad you called, Elaine. I really needed to talk to someone. / Oh, well, hey, I dated Jerry too. I know what a monster he can be. / More wine and turkey? / Who's he? / Oh, he's nobody. / Hey, listen, let me top that off for you.
jokes.setup: The final scene: Elaine is now socializing with Celia.
jokes.punchline: George is serving wine and turkey — the knockout combo — to an unsuspecting Celia. The cycle of drugging-for-toys is beginning again, now with Elaine as accomplice. 'He's nobody' dismisses George as the waiter of their scheme.
jokes.explanation: The episode's dark final button: the drugging scheme has now expanded to include Elaine, and they're repeating it on the very victim who confronted them on TV. Celia's own complaint about Jerry ('I know what a monster he can be') is immediately undercut by her being set up again. The callback to 'more wine and turkey?' from the drugging scenes is perfect and disturbing. A satisfying dark-comedy coda that is fully Seinfeld in its amorality.

tmdb_overview: Kramer turns his apartment into a talk-show set; Jerry's girlfriend won't let him play with her toys; George's clash with pigeons leads to problems with a squirrel; a new employee's silent movement rattles Elaine.
insight: Merv Griffin obsession drives 59 jokes in under 21 minutes, but emotional stakes stay thin.
